meta.title: C257 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes C257 engine — M264, M256, OM654, OM656. Real UK costs, reliability data, and the M256 inline‑six that defines the CLS. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C257 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C257 engine typically costs £2,500–£8,500 supply only, depending on the engine — the OM654 diesel sits at £2,800–£4,800, while the M256 petrol is £3,500–£6,500 and the AMG M256 is £4,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which C257 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 2.0-litre diesel (CLS 220 d) is the most reliable choice — it's Mercedes-developed, efficient, and has a strong track record when serviced properly. The M256 3.0-litre inline-six petrol is also very refined and dependable, though its EQ Boost system adds complexity.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the C257?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can suffer from injector seal leaks (known as 'Black Death'); the M256 petrol engine can develop coolant leaks and EQ Boost system issues; and the OM656 inline-six diesel can experience AdBlue/SCR system failures. Regular servicing is key.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C257 reliable overall?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the C257 is generally well-regarded for reliability, especially with the OM654 diesel and M264/M256 petrol engines. The OM656 inline-six diesel is also reliable but more complex due to its twin-turbo and AdBlue systems. All engines benefit from regular, attentive servicing.

mainEntity.name: Which C257 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the OM656 high-output diesel (CLS 400 d) was a short-production variant (2018–2019) and parts may be harder to source. The AMG CLS 53 is not an engine to avoid, but buyers should be aware of the higher running costs. Any C257 without documented service history should be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C257 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Warning signs include a ticking noise from the engine bay (OM654 injector seals), coolant loss (M256 water pump), AdBlue warnings (OM656 SCR system), or EQ Boost system errors (M256). If you notice any of these, have the car inspected immediately — ignoring them can lead to a full engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C257 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. A well-kept C257 is worth £15,000–£40,000, so a £2,800–£4,800 OM654 recon is only 7–32% of its value — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. Against a higher-mileage CLS 350 d with the OM656, the same maths is still favourable (8–40%). The C257's strong retained values make most engine replacements economically viable.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C257 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can easily exceed 150,000 miles with regular servicing and early attention to the injector seals. The M256 inline-six petrol is similarly durable, though its EQ Boost system may require attention at higher mileages. The OM656 inline-six diesel can also reach high mileages with proper care.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C257 and the previous C218?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C257 is the third-generation CLS, built on the newer MRA platform (shared with the E-Class W213). It introduced a new range of electrified engines (M264, M256, OM654, OM656) with EQ Boost mild-hybrid technology, improved interior quality, and updated technology. The C218 (2010–2018) used older M276 and OM642 engines and is covered on its own generation page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C257 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen), on the vehicle documentation, and stamped on the engine block. For the C257, common codes are M264 (petrol, 2.0L), M256 (petrol, 3.0L), OM654 (diesel, 2.0L), and OM656 (diesel, 2.9L). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes CLS C257 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the third-generation CLS four-door coupé — from the 2.0-litre M264 petrol to the 3.0-litre M256 inline-six and the OM656 diesel. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the EQ Boost mild-hybrid system that redefined the luxury coupé. Backed by 520+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CLS C257 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C257 specifically — including the M264 four-cylinder petrol, the M256 inline-six petrol, and the OM654/OM656 diesel range.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * OM654.920: 95% confidence — inherited from the Model Page's verified figures for the wider Mercedes range. * M256.930: 90% confidence — well‑documented Mercedes inline-six; pattern data. * M264.920: 85% confidence — well‑regarded M264; pattern data. * OM656.929: 80% confidence — inline-six diesel; pattern data. * OM654 M (300 d): 75% confidence — facelift engine; limited long‑term data.

overview.intro: The C257 is the third generation of the Mercedes‑Benz CLS, a four‑door coupé launched in 2018 as the successor to the C218. Built on the Mercedes MRA platform shared with the E‑Class (W213), it introduced a new range of electrified engines — from the 2.0‑litre M264 four‑cylinder petrol to the 3.0‑litre M256 inline‑six petrol and the OM656 inline‑six diesel, all featuring EQ Boost mild‑hybrid technology (48V) for improved efficiency and response. A facelift arrived in 2021, bringing a new OM654 M mild‑hybrid diesel for the CLS 300 d. Production ended in August 2023.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA (shared with E‑Class W213, S‑Class W222) • Predecessor: C218 (2010–2018) • Successor: none (CLS discontinued after C257) • Sibling body styles in this generation: none (CLS is a standalone four‑door coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C257 CLS generated 520+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM654 diesel as the most‑requested powertrain.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel, introduced in 2016, is the pick of the C257 diesel range — efficient, refined, and backed by a strong reliability record when serviced properly.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel for high‑mileage cruising.

items.quote: Mercedes' 3.0‑litre inline‑six petrol, with 362 bhp and EQ Boost mild‑hybrid assistance, delivers effortless power and silky refinement. It's the defining engine of the C257 generation.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the full luxury coupé experience.

items.quote: The inline‑six diesel is generally reliable but complex; its twin‑turbo system and AdBlue/SCR components can be costly to repair if neglected. The CLS 400 d was a short‑production variant, making parts potentially harder to source.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six‑cylinder diesel refinement but can afford the premium maintenance.

items.engine: M256.930 (CLS 450 / AMG CLS 53) — EQ Boost system and turbo
items.quote: The 48V mild‑hybrid system and turbocharger on the M256 can be costly to repair if issues arise. The AMG CLS 53's higher output adds further complexity.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance and can afford the premium running costs.

items.quote: The entry‑level diesel offers the best balance of performance (194 bhp), economy (up to 51 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the C257 range.

The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol with EQ Boost delivers 299 bhp and genuine performance (0‑62 mph in 6.0 seconds). It's often overlooked in favour of the six‑cylinder but offers a compelling blend of performance and efficiency.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol power without the six‑cylinder running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: EQ Boost system, turbo wear, coolant leaks
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — silky inline‑six, premium costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Turbo wear, cooling system, EQ Boost
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Tax — high performance, high running costs.

economicsRule.title: The C257 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£4,800 OM654 recon against a £15,000–£40,000 C257 sits at 7–32% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. Against a higher‑mileage CLS 350 d with the OM656, the same maths is still favourable (8–40%). Value the car first, but the C257's strong retained values make most engine replacements economically viable.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C257 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLS 220 d (OM654) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C257 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AMG CLS 53 (M256) — high‑performance running costs add up.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C257 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLS 220 d (OM654) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: OM654 Injector Seal Leaks (Black Death)
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on the OM654 can fail, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injector. This creates a carbon buildup that sounds like a ticking noise and can eventually damage the cylinder head.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a ticking noise from the engine bay, have the injector seals checked immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows if the head is damaged.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your C257 diesel →

problems.title: M256 EQ Boost System & Coolant Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M256's 48V mild‑hybrid system and coolant system components can develop issues over time — water pump failures and coolant leaks are the most common reported problems.
problems.recommendation: On CLS 450 and AMG CLS 53 models, have the coolant system and EQ Boost components inspected at 60,000 miles — a £600–£1,200 repair is cheap insurance against a £3,500–£6,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M256 engine prices for your C257 petrol →

problems.title: OM656 AdBlue/SCR System Issues
problems.rootCause: The OM656's AdBlue and SCR (selective catalytic reduction) system can suffer from sensor failures and crystallisation, leading to warning lights and potential limp‑home mode.
problems.recommendation: If your CLS 350 d or 400 d shows an AdBlue warning, have the system inspected immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if the system fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare OM656 engine prices for your C257 diesel →

replacementCosts.h2: C257 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C257 four‑door coupé only. The CLS was discontinued after the C257 generation, with no direct successor planned.

2. M256 (3.0 inline‑six petrol) — strong demand for the CLS 450
